race.name: Sportful Dolomiti Race
race.tagline: Five Dolomite passes. 5,000 meters of climbing. Passo Manghen at 2,047m. The Dolomites at their most relentless, through UNESCO World Heritage stone cathedrals.

terrain_types: Sustained gradient climbs with steep sections
vitals.registration: Online via sportfuldolomitirace.it. Cost: EUR55-75. Registration typically opens late autumn prior.
vitals.aid_stations: Feed stations at major col summits and valley checkpoints. Water refill points on longer climbs.
vitals.cutoff_time: Intermediate cutoffs enforced at key checkpoints. Broom wagon support for riders outside time limits.
distance_options: Granfondo: ~210 km (130 mi), ~5,000 m gain — the full event with all 5 passes
Mediofondo: ~130 km — a shorter loop with fewer passes

climate.description: Mid-June in the Dolomites is a shoulder season: warm enough in the valleys (22-28C / 72-82F) but genuinely cold at 2,000m+ summits. The Dolomites are notorious for rapid weather changes — clear mornings can become violent thunderstorms by midday. Snow on high passes in June is not unprecedented. The Manghen at 2,047m can be 10-15C cooler than the start in Feltre, with exposed summits amplifying wind chill.
challenges: Manghen summit (2,047m): 8-12C with wind — the Dolomites' exposed upper reaches
Mid-June timing: shoulder season means snow on high passes is possible
Dolomite thunderstorms build rapidly — lightning at altitude is a genuine hazard
Temperature swing of 15-20C between Feltre valley and Manghen summit

terrain.surface: Italian provincial roads through the Dolomites. All tarmac. Road surfaces range from excellent on major passes to occasionally rough on secondary approaches. The Manghen road is narrower than the tourist passes.
features: Passo Manghen (2,047m) — the queen stage: 18 km from the east at 7.5% average with sections exceeding 12%. One of the toughest climbs in the Eastern Alps.
Passo Rolle (1,984m) — classic Giro d'Italia pass with views of the Pale di San Martino
Passo Valles (2,032m) — quiet Dolomite pass connecting the Val di Fiemme to Falcade
Passo Cereda (1,369m) — short, punchy climb through Dolomite meadows
Croce d'Aune (1,015m) — the final climb, historically significant as the climb Tullio Campagnolo descended in 1927 when he invented the quick-release skewer

gravel_god_rating.tier_note: One of Italy's most prestigious granfondos. Five Dolomite passes including the savage Passo Manghen. UNESCO World Heritage scenery. T2 by score (79). p4 cannot promote into T1.
gravel_god_rating.score_note: elevation=5 for 5,000m across 5 passes. adventure=5 and experience=5 for the Dolomite UNESCO landscape. cultural_impact=3 for Italian cycling heritage including the Campagnolo connection at Croce d'Aune.

logistics.explanation: Feltre is a small Veneto town at the southern foot of the Dolomites. The nearest major airports are Venice (VCE/TSF, 1.5 hours) and Verona (2 hours). The town is well-organized for the event but has limited hotel capacity for 4,000-5,000 riders. The event organization is professional — decades of refinement show in the feed station placement, timing, and support. Getting to Feltre requires some commitment, but it's more accessible than deep Alpine locations.

length.explanation: 210 km with 5,000m of climbing is a full-day mountain ordeal. Most riders finish in 8-11 hours. The route is relentlessly mountainous — five major passes with minimal flat sections. The combination of distance and sustained climbing creates a challenge that is harder than the raw numbers suggest, because the Dolomite passes don't let you recover between efforts. The Mediofondo option (~130 km) exists for riders who want the Dolomite experience without the full 210 km commitment.

technicality.explanation: All paved roads, but the Dolomite descents demand respect — some are steep, with tight switchbacks through mountain villages. The Manghen descent in particular is narrow and fast. The steep climbing sections (12%+ on the Manghen) require good technique: smooth pedaling, weight management, and traction control on steep grades. This is primarily a climbing event on paved roads, but the Dolomite terrain adds a layer of technical awareness that flat sportives lack.

elevation.explanation: 5,000m of climbing across five major passes in 210 km. The climbing density is enormous — nearly 24 meters of gain per kilometer of distance. The Manghen alone delivers 1,400m of climbing at a savage 7.5% average. Rolle, Valles, Cereda, and Croce d'Aune each add 600-1,100m. By the time you crest the final pass, your legs have been in climbing mode for the vast majority of the day. This is among the heaviest climbing loads in European sportive cycling.

climate.explanation: Mid-June Dolomites is a gamble. On good days, you get warm valleys and crisp mountain air. On bad days, you get thunderstorms, hail, and near-freezing conditions at 2,000m. The Dolomites are more weather-volatile than the Western Alps — the unique geology creates its own microclimate patterns. Snow on the Manghen in mid-June has happened. But the highest point (2,047m) is lower than Gavia or Great St. Bernard, so the altitude-driven cold is less extreme.

altitude.explanation: Three passes above 1,900m: Manghen (2,047m), Valles (2,032m), and Rolle (1,984m). Extended time above 1,500m throughout the day. The altitude is meaningful — riders from sea level will feel the reduced oxygen on the upper reaches of the Manghen — but it's not the 2,600m+ world of the Gavia or Galibier. The altitude score reflects genuine but not extreme physiological challenge.

adventure.explanation: The Dolomites are, objectively, the most dramatic mountain landscape in European cycling. The UNESCO World Heritage designation exists for a reason: these are not ordinary mountains but vertical stone cathedrals — pale limestone towers, pinnacles, and walls that rise from green meadows with an almost surreal verticality. Riding through this landscape for 210 km, crossing five passes, is one of cycling's great visual adventures. The Manghen, in particular, passes through terrain that feels like another planet.

prestige.explanation: One of Italy's most prestigious granfondos, alongside the Maratona dles Dolomites and Nove Colli. The Sportful Dolomiti Race carries weight in Italian cycling circles — finishing the full Granfondo with five passes is a genuine achievement. The Manghen is respected as one of the Eastern Alps' toughest climbs. Internationally, it's less well-known than the Maratona, but among riders who know their Dolomite granfondos, this is the harder route.

race_quality.explanation: Decades of Italian granfondo organizational expertise. Professional chip timing, well-stocked feed stations at strategic points, medical support on major cols, and broom wagon coverage. The event handles 4,000-5,000 riders efficiently. Competitive classifications by age category provide genuine racing structure. The Italian granfondo tradition — where events are simultaneously races and celebrations — is strong here.

experience.explanation: Five Dolomite passes through UNESCO World Heritage scenery. The Manghen is the centerpiece — 18 km of relentless climbing through a landscape that shifts from forest to Alpine meadow to barren rock. The summit at 2,047m opens to a panorama of the Dolomite massif that stops you in your tracks even when your legs are screaming. Passo Rolle offers views of the Pale di San Martino — one of the most striking Dolomite groups. And the Croce d'Aune descent — where Campagnolo invented the quick-release — adds a layer of cycling history to every pedal stroke.

community.explanation: Strong Italian granfondo community with a growing international contingent. The event draws heavily from the Veneto, Trentino, and Lombardy regions. The Italian granfondo culture — pasta at the finish, wine in the evening, animated recounting of the day's suffering — is fully present. The community is warm but the international reach is more limited than the Maratona dles Dolomites or Italian events that attract pan-European fields.

field_depth.explanation: Italian club racers and regional elite amateurs populate the front of the field. The competitive depth is genuine — the classification system is taken seriously by the sharp-end riders. The 4,000-5,000 rider field spans a wide ability range, from competitive racers chasing podiums to enthusiasts riding for the Dolomite experience. The difficulty of the route ensures that the field is self-selecting for reasonably fit cyclists.

value.explanation: EUR55-75 for 210 km across five Dolomite passes with professional organization, chip timing, feed stations, and medical support is excellent value. Italian granfondos consistently offer strong value propositions compared to French or Swiss equivalents. For the quality of the Dolomite scenery — UNESCO World Heritage, no less — and the caliber of the climbing, this is one of the best-value mountain sportives in Europe.

expenses.explanation: Feltre is a modest Veneto town — accommodation is more affordable than Dolomite resort towns like Cortina or Madonna di Campiglio. Venice airport is 1.5 hours away with budget airline options. Total trip cost from the US is EUR800-1,500. Within Europe, this is a mid-range sportive in terms of total expenses — the Veneto location offers better value than the more touristic Dolomite destinations.

biased_opinion.summary: The Sportful Dolomiti Race is the Dolomites at their most honest: five passes, 5,000 meters of climbing, and no hiding behind a famous name. The Maratona dles Dolomites gets the headlines and the 30,000-person lottery. The Sportful gets the riders who want the harder route and don't need a lottery to feel validated. The Passo Manghen — 18 km at 7.5% with ramps above 12% — is the kind of climb that separates cyclists who ride mountains from cyclists who ride hills. And the scenery? The Dolomites are not mountains in the normal sense. They are vertical limestone cathedrals — UNESCO gave them World Heritage status for a reason. Riding through the Pale di San Martino from Passo Rolle is one of cycling's great visual moments. The Croce d'Aune, where Campagnolo was so frustrated by frozen wing nuts in 1927 that he invented the quick-release skewer, is a reminder that cycling history was written in these mountains.
strengths: Passo Manghen: 18 km at 7.5% with 12%+ ramps — one of the Eastern Alps' most savage climbs
Five major Dolomite passes in a single day: Manghen, Rolle, Valles, Cereda, Croce d'Aune
UNESCO World Heritage Dolomite scenery — the Pale di San Martino from Passo Rolle is otherworldly
The Campagnolo connection: Croce d'Aune is where the quick-release skewer was born
weaknesses: Mid-June Dolomite weather is volatile — snow on high passes is not unprecedented
Less international recognition than the Maratona dles Dolomites despite being the harder route
Feltre's limited infrastructure strains under 4,000-5,000 riders
biased_opinion.bottom_line: If the Maratona is the Dolomites' party, the Sportful is the Dolomites' exam — harder climbing, fewer riders, and the same UNESCO scenery without the lottery.

final_verdict.one_liner: Five Dolomite passes. 5,000m of climbing. The Manghen at 2,047m. UNESCO stone cathedrals. Italy's toughest granfondo.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want the hardest Dolomite granfondo with fewer riders and no lottery. Yes if the Passo Manghen's 12%+ ramps and UNESCO scenery speak to your cycling soul. Skip if you want the spectacle and lottery drama of the Maratona. Skip if mid-June Dolomite weather volatility concerns you.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want the Dolomites with bigger spectacle: try Maratona dles Dolomites. If you want steeper Italian gradients: try GF Gavia e Mortirolo. If you want French Alpine prestige: try La Marmotte.

logistics.airport: Venice Marco Polo (VCE) — 1.5 hours. Venice Treviso (TSF) — 1.5 hours (budget airlines). Verona (VRN) — 2 hours. Innsbruck (INN) — 3 hours via Brenner Pass.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Stay in Feltre (start/finish) or nearby Belluno (20 min). Feltre is a charming historic town but small — book 3+ months in advance. Belluno offers more capacity. Avoid Dolomite resort towns (Cortina, etc.) — they're too far from the start and overpriced for this event.
logistics.food: Veneto and Dolomite cuisine — polenta, game, mushrooms, local cheeses, excellent wines. Feltre has solid trattorias for pre-ride carb loading. Feed stations provide classic Italian granfondo fare: panini, fruit, cakes, cola, water.
logistics.packet_pickup: Event area in Feltre, typically Saturday before the Sunday race.
logistics.parking: Town parking in Feltre. Manageable for 4,000-5,000 riders but arrive early.

history.origin_story: Born in the Dolomites in the early 1990s, the Sportful Dolomiti Race was created to showcase the most dramatic mountain landscape in Italian cycling. The route was designed around the Passo Manghen — one of the Eastern Alps' toughest climbs, rarely used by the Giro d'Italia but known to local riders as a monster. The inclusion of Passo Rolle (a Giro classic), Passo Valles, Passo Cereda, and the historically significant Croce d'Aune creates a comprehensive Dolomite traverse. The event grew into one of Italy's most respected granfondos, known for its climbing severity and stunning scenery.
notable_moments: Passo Manghen established as one of the hardest climbs in Italian amateur cycling
Croce d'Aune: the descent where Tullio Campagnolo invented the quick-release skewer in 1927, frustrated by frozen wing nuts in a winter race
The Dolomites received UNESCO World Heritage status in 2009, confirming what cyclists already knew — this is Earth's most dramatic mountain scenery
Growth to 4,000-5,000 riders as one of Italy's hardest and most prestigious granfondos
history.reputation: Italy's toughest granfondo. What the Maratona dles Dolomites is in spectacle, the Sportful Dolomiti Race is in pure climbing difficulty. The Manghen alone makes this event legendary in Italian cycling.

course_description.character: Relentless Dolomite climbing through the most dramatic mountain scenery in European cycling. Five major passes in 210 km — the route is essentially a linked series of climbs and descents with minimal flat. The Manghen is the queen stage: 18 km of sustained 7.5% with ramps above 12% through increasingly wild terrain to a 2,047m summit. The other four passes — Rolle, Valles, Cereda, Croce d'Aune — each deliver 600-1,100m of climbing through UNESCO World Heritage landscape. The character is distinctly Italian: suffering, beauty, and history intertwined.

suffering_zones.label: Passo Manghen
suffering_zones.desc: The queen stage. 18 km from the east side at 7.5% average with sections exceeding 12%. The climb begins in forest, passes through Alpine meadows, and finishes on exposed rock at 2,047m. The sustained gradient is the challenge — there are no flat recovery sections within the climb. By the time you reach the steepest ramps above 1,800m, altitude compounds the gradient. The summit panorama of the Dolomite massif is the reward.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: 2,047m summit, 18 km at 7.5% average, 12%+ maximum sections

suffering_zones.label: Passo Rolle
suffering_zones.desc: Classic Giro d'Italia pass at 1,984m. The approach from the Val di Fiemme is steady and scenic, with the Pale di San Martino — the most visually striking Dolomite group — dominating the skyline. The summit sits beneath these limestone towers, creating one of cycling's great photo opportunities. The climb is honest rather than brutal, but on legs already carrying the Manghen, the sustained 6-7% gradients accumulate.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: 1,984m summit, classic Giro climb with Pale di San Martino views

suffering_zones.label: Passo Valles
suffering_zones.desc: The third 2,000m+ pass at 2,032m. A quiet Dolomite crossing that connects the Val di Fiemme to Falcade. Less well-known than Rolle or Manghen, the Valles delivers steady climbing through remote Dolomite terrain. By this point — pass number three, 140+ km — the accumulated climbing begins to tell. The descent into Falcade offers a brief recovery before the final two climbs.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: 2,032m summit, steady climbing through quiet Dolomite terrain

suffering_zones.label: Croce d'Aune
suffering_zones.desc: The final climb at 1,015m — short and historically significant. This is where Tullio Campagnolo, descending in a winter race in 1927, was so frustrated by frozen wing nuts that he conceived the quick-release skewer. Every cyclist who rides over the Croce d'Aune passes through a piece of cycling history. On tired legs with 180 km and 4,500m already banked, even a modest climb becomes a genuine effort.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: 1,015m summit, birthplace of the quick-release skewer

course_description.signature_challenge: The Passo Manghen defines this event: 18 km at 7.5% average with ramps above 12%, through progressively wilder terrain to a 2,047m summit with panoramic Dolomite views. Combined with four more passes — including two others above 1,900m — the total 5,000m of climbing across the Dolomite landscape makes this one of cycling's great mountain days.

guide_variables.race_terrain: Paved Dolomite mountain passes, 5 major climbs
guide_variables.race_weather: Warm valleys (72-82F), cool at 2,000m+ summits (8-12C). Dolomite weather is volatile — thunderstorms and even snow possible in June.
race_challenges: Passo Manghen: 18 km at 7.5% average, 12%+ maximum — the queen stage
5,000m of climbing across 5 major Dolomite passes in 210 km
Three passes above 1,900m: Manghen (2,047m), Valles (2,032m), Rolle (1,984m)
Mid-June Dolomite weather volatility — prepare for anything

non_negotiables.requirement: Mountain climbing endurance: multiple 3-4 hour climbing sessions per week
non_negotiables.why: Five major Dolomite passes in 210 km demand sustained climbing endurance. The Manghen alone is 60-90 minutes of non-stop climbing. You need the specific muscular endurance to maintain power output across repeated 30-60 minute climbs.

non_negotiables.requirement: Steep gradient preparation: repeated efforts at 8-12% for 15+ minutes
non_negotiables.why: The Manghen's 12%+ ramps after 18 km of 7.5% average demand specific steep-climbing preparation. Find your steepest local climb and repeat it. If nothing exceeds 8%, do over-geared intervals to simulate the muscular load.

non_negotiables.requirement: Full dress rehearsal: 160+ km with 3,000m+ climbing and tested nutrition
non_negotiables.why: 210 km with 5,000m of climbing requires proven nutrition strategy, pacing discipline, and the confidence that your body can handle 8-11 hours of sustained mountain riding. The dress rehearsal reveals problems before race day.

videos.title: LA NOSTRA GIORNATA PIÙ DURA IN BICI DA STRADA ALLA SPORTFUL DOLOMITI RACE
videos.description:
La nostra giornata più dura in bici da strada alla Sportful Dolomiti Race

In questo video pedaliamo e vi raccontiamo la Sportful Dolomiti Race 2025, percorso Granfondo.

Partenza e arrivo: Feltre (BL)
Lunghezza: 204km x 5100mt
Tempo percorrenza: 8h 20min (tempo in gara)

Partenza da Feltre alle 7:00. Si percorre: Forcella Franche, Passo Staulanza, Passo Duran, Forcella Aurine, Passo Cereda e Croce d’Aune. Il percorso è stupendo, si pedala tra le Dolomiti, passando per Agordo, Alleghe, Caprile, Selva di Cadore e Fiera di Primiero. Tanti ristori, posizionati ad ogni GPM. Organizzazione impeccabile.

La traccia la trovi sul nostro profilo komoot ‘Gravellata’

Abbiamo fatto anche la Maratona Dles Dolomites: https://youtu.be/iZZDMs9R2b8

Ci vediamo al prossimo video!

keep pedaling

#ciclismo #roadbike #sportfuldolomitirace
videos.curation_reason: Recent high-quality race recap from 2025 with detailed course coverage and strong viewership.

un evento da provare.   #NONOSTANTE TUTTO..,  FINO ALLA FINE !"

- PASSO MANGHEN "nella tana del lupo "
- PASSO ROLLE "al cospetto delle Dolomiti"
